title: Resource Partitioning in Warblers
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive MicroSim showing how five warbler species partition foraging zones on a conifer tree to reduce interspecific competition.

learningObjectives: Compare how different species partition resources within a shared habitat to reduce interspecific competition
Explain the competitive exclusion principle and how resource partitioning allows coexistence
Identify niche dimensions along which species can partition resources
Connect MacArthur's warbler study to broader ecological principles
prerequisites: Understanding of competition as an ecological interaction
Basic vocabulary: niche, habitat, species, competition
Familiarity with food webs and trophic relationships

keyQuestions: How do five similar warbler species coexist on the same tree?
What would happen if one warbler species went extinct?
What are the three main dimensions of resource partitioning?
How does resource partitioning relate to biodiversity?
commonMisconceptions: Species with overlapping habitats always compete directly for the same resources
Resource partitioning requires species to eat completely different foods
The competitive exclusion principle means only one species can live in any habitat
assessmentOpportunities: Students identify which warbler species pairs have the most overlap and predict competition intensity
Students create resource partitioning diagrams for other ecosystems
Students explain why removing one species would affect the others
